coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive and selective photoelectrochemical aptasensing of di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate based on graphene quantum dots decorated TiO2 nanotube arrays
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Yuan
Yan, Wenjun
Guo, Yujing
Wang, Qiang
Bi, Yingpu
Dong, Chuan
Fan, Lifang
coredata.dc:description: A photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform for di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP) was constructed using graphene quantum dots decorated TiO2 nanotube arrays (GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs) as the transducer species and the anti-DEHP aptamer as the biological recognition element. GQDs were synthesized using the alkali-mediated hydrothermal method, and then anchored onto the TiO2 NTs uniformly and intimately via pronounced electrostatic interaction. Coupling GQDs with TiO2 NTs not only enhanced visible-light absorption, but promoted charge separation and transportation, exhibiting excellent photocurrent response, and PEC activity. Various means were conducted to explore morphologies, optical, structural and PEC properties of the materials. As an identification unit, the anti-DEHP aptamer molecules were immobilized on GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs using a cross-linking coupling method. The developed PEC sensing platform exhibits excellent sensing behavior for DEHP, and provides a low detection limit of 0.1 ng/L, high selectivity and stability. Meanwhile, its application in real environmental samples was evaluated and satisfying results were achieved. Thus, the established sensing platform provides a promising tool to detect DEHP in the environment.

(A) XRD patterns of GQDs, TiO2 NTs, and GQDs-decorated TiO2; (B) Raman spectra of TiO2 NTs and GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs; (C) DRS spectra of TiO2 NTs and GQDs-decorated TiO2, inset is the corresponding Tauc plots; (D) PL spectra of GQDs and GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs.

(A) Photocurrent responses in 0.1 M PBS (PH 7.4) and (B) EIS diagrams of TiO2 NTs (a), GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs (b), After immobilization of aptamer (c), after BSA blocking (d) after addition of DEHP (e) in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl.

(A) Photocurrent responses of the PEC sensing platform in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) with different concentrations of DEHP (from a to l: 0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 10, 30, 50, 100, 300 ng/L) under visible irradiation; (B) Photocurrent density change against DEHP concentrations, Inset: the corresponding linear relationship between the photocurrent change and logarithm of DEHP concentrations ranging from 0.3 to 100 ng/L; Chronocoulometric responses of aptamer-immobilized GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs (C) and GQDs-loaded FTO (D) in the absence (a) and presence (b) of [Ru (NH3)6]3+ (1 mM).

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive and selective photoelectrochemical aptasensing of di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate based on graphene quantum dots decorated TiO2 nanotube arrays

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform for di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP) was constructed using graphene quantum dots decorated TiO2 nanotube arrays (GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs) as the transducer species and the anti-DEHP aptamer as the biological recognition element. GQDs were synthesized using the alkali-mediated hydrothermal method, and then anchored onto the TiO2 NTs uniformly and intimately via pronounced electrostatic interaction. Coupling GQDs with TiO2 NTs not only enhanced visible-light absorption, but promoted charge separation and transportation, exhibiting excellent photocurrent response, and PEC activity. Various means were conducted to explore morphologies, optical, structural and PEC properties of the materials. As an identification unit, the anti-DEHP aptamer molecules were immobilized on GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs using a cross-linking coupling method. The developed PEC sensing platform exhibits excellent sensing behavior for DEHP, and provides a low detection limit of 0.1 ng/L, high selectivity and stability. Meanwhile, its application in real environmental samples was evaluated and satisfying results were achieved. Thus, the established sensing platform provides a promising tool to detect DEHP in the environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Phthalic acid esters (PAEs), one of typical environmental contaminants, are commonly used as plasticizers to impart flexibility to plastic products (Chen et al., 2019; Net et al., 2015; X. Li et al., 2019; S. Li et al., 2019). Due to their large-scale use in people's daily life, such as food packaging materials, medical devices and children's toy materials, PAEs have become the most common man-made aromatic chemicals (Das et al., 2020). Especially, di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP), one of the most widely used plasticizers, is insoluble in water and has been placed on the priority pollutant list of European Union (EU), the United States Environmental Protection Agency (U.S. EPA) and China (Bernard et al., 2014). DEHP is easy to escape from plastic products, spreading to different environmental media including air, soil, drinking water and food products, because DEHP is physically combined with plastic polymers rather than chemically. Furthermore, DEHP could enrich in body tissues through the respiratory system, swallowing and skin contact, affecting the endocrine system and reproductive system and increasing the incidence of carcinogenicity or tumorigenicity (Xu et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2020). Thus, it is urgently required to explore a facile and efficient method to detect DEHP for safeguarding the environment and public health.

Currently, various methods have been developed for DEHP detection, including gas chromatography with a flame ionization detector (GC-FID) (Tahmasebi et al., 2016; Sargazi et al., 2017), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (Özer et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2018), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Makkliang et al., 2017; Cheng et al., 2017; Akkbik et al., 2019). Although these methods enable accurate and reliable quantification and identification of DEHP, they usually require bulky equipment, special extraction techniques and well-trained operators. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) (Sun and Zhuang, 2014) have been developed for detection of DEHP showing high selectivity. However, these immunoassays need costly antibodies and enzymes as the biorecognition elements, and they are not stable enough under harsh test conditions. Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) technique (Yang et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2019) possesses simple operation, rapid response and high sensitivity, but it usually involves complex mechanisms and the acquisition of SERS signal relies on nano-scale rough metal. These drawbacks limit their wide applications.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) method, as a new analytical technique, possesses the merits of easy operation, fast response, automated instruments and real-time detection. In particular, due to the complete separation of the input (light) and output (electricity) signals, PEC technique has been recognized to be an ultrasensitive analytical method. However, it lacks of selectivity since most other environmental contaminants can be oxidized simultaneously by strong oxidants like superoxide (O2 ·
- ), hydroxyl (HO·) and hydroperoxyl (HO2 ·) radicals produced by light irradiation during PEC tests. Therefore, it is important to find some substances to effectively improve the selectivity of the PEC method. Aptamers are short oligonucleotide sequences, which can bind their targets with high affinity and specificity. Moreover, compared to antibodies, aptamers are stable, simple to modify, and flexible to design the sequence (Han et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018). So, PEC sensing platforms based on aptamer as the biorecognition element are undoubtedly very promising candidates for detecting analytes selectively.

As known, the PEC electrode is critical for the development of a PEC sensing platform to achieve prominent analytical performance. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is the most promising semiconductor material for PEC biosensing due to its low cost, good biocompatibility, non-toxicity and chemical stability (Murali et al., 2020; Li et al., 2015). Among all of nanosized morphologies of TiO2, highly ordered 1D TiO2 NTs self-growing from Ti substrates are appropriate for PEC sensing because of their good biocompatibility and large specific surface area (Gao et al., 2020), which can not only provide a suitable microenvironment for keeping biomolecules good bioactivity, but increase the load capacity of biomolecules. It is very promising in the biosensing analytical application. However, due to the wide band gap (3.2 eV), and the high recombination rate of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, its application is still limited. To overcome these disadvantages, coupling with narrow-band gap semiconductors is an ideal choice to increase visible absorption and promote charge separation (Wang et al., 2017). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which belong to quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials, show unique properties of both graphene and QDs (Ganganboina et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2015). Compared with the conventional semiconductor QDs, GQDs exhibit more advantages like non-toxicity and photostability. Meanwhile, due to their zero-band gap, short charge-transfer pathway and high electron mobility, the recombination of photogenerated charges is attenuated (Fei et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018). Additionally, the light absorption properties of GQDs make them highly promising for remarkable photoresponse and high-gain PEC detection capabilities in a broad wavelength range (Mihalache et al., 2017; Sweetman et al., 2019). Especially, owing to the large surface area and outstanding biocompatibility, GQDs can provide an excellent microenvironment for immobilizing biomolecules, which is a promising prospect in biomedical and sensing applications (Tsai et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2020; Kalkal et al., 2020; X. Li et al., 2019; S. Li et al., 2019). So far, GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs composites have been mainly explored for photocatalytic and photoelectrocatalytic applications (Gupta et al., 2015；Pan et al., 2013). However, to the best of our knowledge, there are few reports on GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs acting as PEC sensing materials.

In this work, GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs composites were synthesized to enact a substrate of PEC sensing platform. The GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs show strong light absorption capability, excellent photocurrent response and PEC activity. Herein, di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP) was chosen as a model molecule, and the anti-DEHP aptamer molecules were immobilized on GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs by a facile cross-linking coupling method. Due to the high affinity and specificity of aptamer to DEHP, the formed aptamer-DEHP complex with poor conductivity increases the hindrance of electron transfer, and decreases in photocurrent density. Quantitative analysis for DEHP was performed based on the correlation between the change of the photocurrent density and DEHP concentrations. The analytical performance of proposed PEC aptasensing for DEHP was studied in detail. Additionally, its application in environmental samples was evaluated.

DEHP, phthalic acid, NaCl, NaOH, NH4F, chitosan, glutaraldehyde and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethylene glycol was obtained from Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Poly (diallyldimethylammonium) chloride (PDDA) was obtained from Sigma-aldrich Co. (US). Pyrene, dibutylphthalate (DBP) and benzyl butyl phthalate (BBP) were purchased from Innochem Company (Beijing, China). PCB72 and PCB77 were acquired from Beijing Lark Company (Beijing, China). Methyl alcohol was offered by Titan Scientific Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The amine-terminated aptamer probe for DEHP was synthesized and provided from Sangon Bioengineering Ltd. Company (Shanghai, China), and their sequences were shown as follows:
5′-NH2-(CH2)6-AACGGAAGCGCGAAGCTTGCGC-3′.

X-ray powder diffractometry (XRD, Bruker D8 Venture, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD, UK) were employed to characterize the crystal structure and element composition of the materials, respectively. The atomic force microscope (AFM) imaging was conducted using the Bruker Dimension ICON microscope in the tapping mode in the atmosphere. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained with UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, U-3010). The UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded by Aligent Cary 8454 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The morphologies of the materials were characterized by the scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan) with an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns were obtained using a JSM-7001F (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. Raman spectroscopic measurements were collected using Horiba LabRAM high resolution UV–Vis-NIR instrument. Zeta potential measurements were carried out with a particle size analyzer (Zeta sizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Ltd, UK). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (Gary Eclipse, Varian). PEC and electrochemical experiments were performed on a CHI660C electrochemical workstation (CHI, Shanghai, China) using a three-electrode system with a synthesized composites as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode and platinum foil acted as the counter electrode. HPLC measurements were performed on Agilent HPLC-1260 chromatograph (HPLC parameters: Column: Agilent Eclipse XDB-C18 (4.6 × 250 mm, 5 µm); UV detection wavelength: 230 nm; column temperature: 30 ℃; mobile phase: methanol and H2O; flow rate: 1 mL/min; injection volume: 20 μL).

TiO2 NTs were prepared by two-step anodization (Xiao et al., 2018). The first oxidation was carried out at 55 V for 1 h in an electrolyte composed of ethylene glycol, 0.25 wt% of NH4F and 2 vol% of H2O. And the product was sonicated in deionized water and dried in the air. Afterwards, anodization was performed again under the same conditions, followed by sonication clean in ethanol. The anodized samples were crystallized at 450 °C for 2 h in the air.

GQDs were synthesized by the alkali-mediated hydrothermal method as described in the literature (Liu et al., 2020). In brief, 2 g pyrene was refluxed in HNO3 (160 mL) with stirring at 80 °C for 12 h. The reaction product was filtered and rinsed with deionized water, and yellow substance, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was obtained. Then, 0.75 g of the product was added in 150 mL of 0.2 M NaOH under 2 h sonication (300 W, 40 kHz). The suspension was transferred to a reactor and settled at 200 °C for 10 h. The reddish-brown solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter to remove impurities and further purified with a dialysis bag (with retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 2 days to remove the residual sodium and small molecules. Eventually, deep-brown GQDs solution was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs composites
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were decorated on TiO2 NTs by electrostatic adsorption according to the reported literature with slight modification (Liu et al., 2015). First, TiO2 NTs were treated with a solution containing 8% PDDA and 0.5 M NaCl to make its surface positively charged. The treated photoelectrode was immersed into the GQDs solution (1 mg/mL) under ambient condition, and dried in N2. Thus, GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs composites were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of aptamer on GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs
ce:section.ce:para: Before preparation, GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs was fully sealed with insulating glue except for a 1.0 cm2 area. 35 μL chitosan solution (1%, w/v) was covered onto the sealed composites with the area of 1.0 cm2, and then 45 μL of 3% glutaraldehyde solution was dropped and reacted for 30 min. Finally, the CHO-functionalized GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs were incubated into 30 μL of 3 μM terminal amino-modified aptamer for 12 h at 4 °C. The aptamer-immobilized GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs were rinsed to remove unbound aptamer, followed by blocked with 30 μL BSA (3%, w/v) to avoid nonspecific adsorption. The PEC sensing platform was prepared and stored at 4 °C for use.

PEC measurements were conducted in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.41) under visible irradiation with an applied potential of 0 V using chronoamperometry. A 300 W xenon lamp Perfect Light (Beijing, China) with a 420 nm cut-off filter was used as the light source. The distance between the irradiation source and the photoelectrode was 30 cm, and the light intensity was 50 mW cm−2. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in a solution containing 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] and 0.1 M KCl in the frequency ranging from 10− 2 Hz to 105 Hz at 5 mV. All potentials were measured versus Ag/AgCl electrode.

For DEHP analysis, the as-prepared PEC sensing platform was immersed in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.41) containing different concentrations of DEHP for 40 min, and the photocurrent densities were recorded by chronoamperometry.

The typical TEM image of the synthesized GQDs is shown in Fig. 1A. It is clearly observed that GQDs with the average sizes of 5.75 ± 1.24 nm were uniformly dispersed without visual aggregation and the corresponding particle size distribution is shown in inset of Fig. 1A. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1B) displays that GQDs exhibit actual lattice fringes, and the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm can be indexed to the (100) plane of graphene (Pan et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 1C, the thickness of GQDs was analyzed by AFM, and their sizes are largely in the range of 7–9 nm, suggesting that the synthesized GQDs are multi-layered. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of GQDs in Fig. 1D shows that there are two absorption peaks at 240 nm and 350 nm. The absorption band at about 240 nm is attributed to the π-π * transition of aromatic sp2 domains and another weak absorption peak at 350 nm corresponds to the n-π * transition of CO (Du et al., 2020; Ebrahimi et al., 2017). Meanwhile, GQDs display a relatively wide light absorption range, which is beneficial to use as a photosensitizer to modify TiO2 NTs. In addition, PL spectra for GQDs were measured (Inset of Fig. 1D). Upon excitation at 486 nm, GQDs show a strong fluorescence peak at 562 nm. It is noteworthy that the emission peak does not move with the excitation wavelength change. The result demonstrates that the sizes and surface state of GQDs exhibit good uniformity, which is consistent with the previous report (Lu et al., 2018). In addition, the surface electrical potential analysis of GQDs is shown in Fig. S1, the zeta potential of GQDs is − 34.5 mV. This result verifies that GQDs is relatively stable because of the absolute value of theζ-potential is over than 30 mV and electrostatic absorption for decoration of GQDs onto the positively charged surface of TiO2 NTs electrode is feasible.

The SEM morphologies of TiO2 NTs are shown in Fig. 2A. It can be seen that highly ordered and compactly arranged TiO2 NTs are grown vertically on the Ti foil, and their average inner diameter and the length are about 120 nm and 13.7 µm, respectively. The as-prepared TiO2 NTs with uniform tubular structure can provide a large surface area to load GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, further modification of GQDs on TiO2 NTs did not cause any morphology changes, which may be the relatively small size of GQDs on TiO2 NTs. The alignment of the nanotubes is also confirmed from the cross-sectional images of TiO2 NTs and GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs in the inset of Fig. 2A and B. The prepared samples were further characterized by TEM. The tubular structure of TiO2 NTs is clearly observed from Fig. 2C. The HRTEM image of the TiO2 NTs in Fig. 2D clearly shows anatase lattice fringes along the (101) direction with a D-spacing of 0.35 nm (Zeng et al., 2018). The SAED pattern of TiO2 NT in the Fig. 2E exhibits spotty ring patterns for TiO2 NT arrays in anatase phase, and A (101), A (004), A (200) and A (204) peaks are confirmed in this figure.

According to the TEM (Fig. 2F) and HRTEM images of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs (Fig. 2G), it could be clearly seen that vast small-sized GQDs with a particle size of about 3 nm are uniformly assembled on TiO2 NTs, and there are no GQDs outside the nanomaterial, indicating that GQDs are indeed tightly attached to the TiO2 NTs surface by electrostatic interaction. The EDX spectrum survey illustrated in Fig. 2H demonstrates the existence of C, O and Ti in the GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs, and the corresponding element mappings confirm that three elements are evenly distributed on the materials. The atomic ratio of prepared GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs is given in Table S1. These results further prove that GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs composites have been prepared successfully.

Chemical composition and surface electronic states of the composites were revealed in the XPS spectra. As displayed in Fig. 3A, the fully XPS survey spectrum further confirms the presence of the elements C, O and Ti in the composites. The peaks of C1s (Fig. 3B) appearing at 284.8 eV, 288.6 eV, and 285.6 eV correspond to CC, CO, and C-C/C-H, respectively (Gupta et al., 2020). As shown in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3C), the main oxygen peak at 530.2 eV is attributed to the Ti-O bond in the TiO2 lattice, while the signal at 531.5 eV indicates the presence of hydroxyl oxygen in Ti-OH (Tang et al., 2018). The Ti 2p spectrum in Fig. 3D is fitted with two strong peaks at 458.9 eV and 464.8 eV, which can be assigned to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of Ti4+ in TiO2 (Feizpoor et al., 2018).

Fig. 4A displays the XRD patterns for the three samples. The diffraction peak of GQDs observed at 26.61° corresponds to the (002) plane of bulk graphite, demonstrating the graphitic nature of the crystalline dots (JCPDS no. 26-1080) (Yu et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2014). As for TiO2 NTs, all of the peaks appearing at 25.3°, 38.0°, 48.0°, 53.9°, 55.1°, 62.8° and 70.5° could be well indexed to the reflection from planes (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204) and (220) of the anatase phase (JCPDS No. 21-1272) (Tang et al., 2018; Gupta et al., 2015), respectively. The other peaks are assigned to Ti metal (JCPDS No. 44-1294) (Zheng et al., 2020). After decorating GQDs on TiO2 NTs, the XRD pattern of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs composites is similar to that of TiO2 NTs, demonstrating that the load of GQDs onto TiO2 NTs does not cause any change in the crystal structure of TiO2 NTs. There is no observable characteristic diffraction peak from GQDs, which may be ascribed to their low content and high dispersion in the composites. This result is well consistent well with the previous literatures (Pan et al., 2015; Chang et al., 2020). Raman spectroscopy is further used to prove the existence of GQDs on the surface of TiO2 NTs. As shown in Fig. 4B, three strong peaks located at about 393, 514 and 632 cm−1 in bare TiO2 NTs, which can be assigned to the B1g, A1g or B1g and Eg modes of anatase TiO2 phase (Murali et al., 2020), respectively. In the case of the composite, besides the three peaks mentioned above, two additional peaks are observed at 1378 and 1599 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands of GQDs (Pan et al., 2015; Subramanian et al., 2020; Li et al., 2015), respectively. These observations confirm that GQDs have been deposited on TiO2 NTs.

The light absorption properties of TiO2 NTs before and after decorated GQDs were investigated using DRS. As shown in Fig. 4C, the absorption edge of TiO2 NTs is around 386 nm. With the modification of GQDs, the absorption edge of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs is significantly red-shifted to 486 nm, showing strong visible-light absorption, which helps to effectively prevent the inactivation of aptamer molecules immobilized on GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs. These results confirm that the presence of GQDs is capable of enhancing the visible absorption of TiO2 NTs greatly and improving the PEC performance of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs. The corresponding bandgap can be estimated from the Tauc plots (inset in Fig. 4C). The linear section of the Tauc plot is extrapolated to (ahυ)1/2 = 0 and the bandgap energies of the TiO2 NTs and GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs are 3.21 eV and 2.54 eV, respectively. According to the literature (Yan et al., 2020; Fan et al., 2021), GQDs have a band gap around 2.31 eV with a valence band of 1.48 eV, and the valence band (VB) position of TiO2 NTs is 2.68 eV. The conduction band position can be calculated as − 0.53 eV and − 0.83 eV for TiO2 NTs and GQDs using equation ECB = EVB- Eg, respectively. Based on the above analyses, the energy band for TiO2 NTs and GQDs, and the plausible electron transfer mechanism of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs was depicted in Scheme 1. Under the visible-light excitation, the electrons of GQDs were photoexcited to its conduction band (CB), forming electron-hole pairs. The holes in the valence band (VB) transferred to the surface of the photoelectrode and participated in redox reaction. The photogenerated electrons were photoinjected the CB of TiO2 NTs due to the CB energy levels of TiO2 NTs being lower than the CB of GQDs, and flowed to the external circuit through Ti foil, generating photocurrent. Such a vectorial charge transfer pathway facilitates the charge separation at the interface, which is crucial for constructing the sensing platform with outstanding analytical performance.

At the same time, PL spectroscopy was used to study the electron-hole recombination behavior of pristine and modified TiO2 NTs, and the results are displayed in Fig. 4D. GQDs exhibit a strong fluorescence peak. While TiO2 NTs decorated with GQDs, the PL intensity of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs is significantly reduced and exhibits a very obvious quenching emission phenomenon, compared to the pristine GQDs. This result confirms that the combination of TiO2 NTs and GQDs can facilitate the separation of electron-hole pairs and prolong the life of photo-generated carriers, which is benefit for improving photocurrent response and PEC activity of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs.

Scheme 2 depicts the preparation procedures and operating mechanism of PEC sensing platform for detection of DEHP. Upright orderly TiO2 NTs were prepared by two-step anodization, and then GQDs were decorated on TiO2 NTs by electrostatic adsorption. Afterwards, chitosan containing numerous functional groups amine (-NH2) was coated on the surface of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs, and then glutaraldehyde was immobilized on the photoelectrode by the interaction between -CHO and -NH2. Thus, the CHO functionalization of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs was obtained. Subsequently, NH2-terminal aptamer molecules were anchored the surface of the CHO-functionalized photoelectrode by covalent bonding between the -NH2 and -CHO. The photocurrent responses for different construction processes of the PEC sensing platform were recorded by Chronoamperometry ( Fig. 5A). A low photocurrent density is observed for TiO2 NTs (curve a). After the modification of GQDs, the photocurrent density increases apparently to 12.96 μA cm−2 (curve b), which could be attributed to the fact that the close and large contacted interfaces between GQDs and TiO2 in the composite improved the visible-light collection capability and inhibited hole-electron recombination. When anchoring the aptamer on the CHO-functionalized GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs, the photocurrent density decreases to 10.06 μA cm−2 (curve c), which could be ascribed to that these biomolecules prohibit electron transfer, causing a negative impact on PEC response. After treated with BSA, the photocurrent density decreases again (curve d). Finally, when the target DEHP was captured on the photoelectrode, the photocurrent density further decreases to 5.67 μA cm−2 (curve e). This reason for decrement of the photocurrent density can be related to the following aspects. On the one hand, owing to the biorecognition reaction between aptamer and its target, DEHP in the test solution could be captured to the sensing interface, and the formation of the DEHP-aptamer complexes with large steric hindrance increased the electron transport resistance, causing an adverse effect on the photocurrent responses. On the other hand, the formed complexes with poor conductivity hindered the diffusion of electrons from the solution to the sensing interface, further decreasing the photocurrent density. Thus, the apparent change of photocurrent densities could be exploited for quantitative detection of DEHP.

EIS measurements were also used to characterize each step of constructing the sensing platform, as displayed in Fig. 5B. As displayed in Inset of Fig. 5B, the fitting circuit includes Warburg impedance (Z w), the double-layer capacitance (C dl), the solution-phase resistance (R s) and the charge transfer resistance (R et). The EIS value of pure TiO2 NTs is 20.96 KΩ (curve a). Apparently, the EIS value dramatically decreases to be 8.27 KΩ (curve b) when GQDs are loaded on TiO2 NTs. This is owing to that the decoration of GQDs on TiO2 NTs improves the conductivity of TiO2 NTs and reduces the rate of charges recombination. After fixing the anti-DEHP aptamer molecules, the EIS value increases to 37.18 KΩ (curve c), which is due to an electrostatic repulsion force between the negatively charged phosphate backbones of aptamer and [Fe(CN)6]3/4-. Similarly, the EIS value increases again to 49.23 KΩ after blocking with BSA (curve d). After the biorecognition reaction, the EIS value obviously increases to 59.42 KΩ (curve e) because the formed DEHP-aptamer complexes inhibit the interface electrons transfer. The EIS results are well in agreement with that of PEC response, representing the sensing platform is successfully constructed for DEHP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical behavior of the proposed PEC sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The photocurrent densities were recorded upon successive additions of DEHP with various concentrations under the optimal conditions. It could be observed from Fig. 6A that the photocurrent densities gradually decrease accompanied by continuing addition of DEHP in the range of 0.1–300 ng/L. This could be explained that vast poorly conductive DEHP-aptamer complexes generated through a mass of DEHP being captured on the sensing surface hinder the electron transfer. When the concentration of DEHP is over 300 ng/L, the photocurrent densities keep almost unchanged, demonstrating DEHP have absolutely interacted with the aptamer on the photoelectrode surface. Fig. 6B exhibits the relationship curve between the change of the photocurrent densities and different concentrations of DEHP, where ΔI is the change value of the photocurrent density before and after adding. ΔI is proportional to the logarithm of DEHP concentrations from 0.3 to 100 ng/L (Inset of Fig. 6B). The linear equation is ΔI = 0.69748 logC + 1.55626 (R2 = 0.9986) and the limit of detection for DEHP is 0.1 ng/L. The proposed sensing platform displays the satisfied results in terms of DEHP detection. Additionally, the comparisons between the proposed and previously reported methods are shown in Table 1. It could be obviously found that the developed sensing platform exhibits better analytical performance toward DEHP than other methods. The detection limit of 0.1 ng/L for DEHP is lower the maximum acceptable limits of 8 μg/L in water recommended by the World health Organization (WHO) (Net et al., 2015). Also, the proposed method is simpler, easier to operate, and using inexpensive instruments. This reason for its excellent sensing performance can be mainly attributed to the following points. First, the appropriate photoactive material TiO2 NTs was explored and decorated by GQDs. The dominant tube structure of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs with large surface area and good biocompatibility not only provides a good microenvironment and large spaces for anchoring the anti-DEHP aptamer, but remains their good bioactivity and strong binding ability towards DEHP, which is beneficial to improve the sensing performance. Besides, the designed GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs composites enhance the visible absorption and accelerate the separation of photogenerated carries, as well as improve the photocurrent response and PEC performance of the photoelectrode, further improving the sensing performance. Most importantly, the high packing density of aptamer on the composites has a great influence on the sensing performance. The packing density was measured by chronocoulometry in the presence of 1 mM [Ru(NH3)6]3+ (Steel et al., 1998), shown in Fig. 6C and it is calculated to be 6.03 × 1013 molecules·cm−2. Here, the control experiments using GQDs-loaded FTO for modifying the anti-DEHP aptamer were also performed (Fig. 6D), and the value is 9.07 × 1012 molecules cm−2. The detailed calculation was supplied in supplementary materials. Apparently, a 6-fold increase relative to the gain of the described that on the GQDs-loaded FTO is achieved. Such high packing density of aptamer greatly improves the sensing performance.

In addition, before determination of DEHP, some parameters that might affect the sensing behavior were examined. First, excellent photocurrent response of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs is very important to achieve the best detection of DEHP, which depends on the concentrations of GQDs and the immerse time of TiO2 NTs in GQDs solution in this work. The concentrations of GQDs solution and the immerse time of TiO2 NTs in GQDs solution effected on the photocurrent response of GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs were optimized to be 1 mg/mL and 9 h, shown in Fig. S2-S3. The effect of various concentrations of aptamer on the sensing behavior were investigated, and the concentration of 3 μM was chosen (Fig. S4). Furthermore, the interaction time between aptamer and DEHP was also optimized to be 40 min (Fig. S5).

The selectivity for the proposed method was investigated by comparing ΔI/I 0 to 5 ng/L DEHP against that to several 50 ng/L interferences including phthalate, PCB77, PCB72, BBP and DBP. As indicated in Fig. 7A, the ΔI/I 0 value is 0.31 in the presence 5 ng/L of DEHP, while it is less than 0.06 for other five interferences with 10-fold concentration. At the same time, the selective experiments were studied by examining ΔI/I 0 in the presence of the mixture containing 5 ng/L DEHP and five interfering substances with the concentration of 50 ng/L. It can be clearly observed that the ΔI/I 0 value is very close to that caused by DEHP only. These results demonstrate that the present method has good selectivity and specificity for DEHP. This is mainly owed to the specificity and high affinity of the aptamer to its target.

In order to achieve the detection in actual environmental samples, it is significant to test the stability of the sensing platform. As shown in Fig. 7B, the photocurrent response remains almost unchanged during multiple cycles of photoexcitation tests within 1000 s, indicating that present sensing platform has excellent stability. After it was stored at 4 °C for two weeks, the photocurrent density remains 96.9% of its initial photocurrent response, confirming that the present sensing platform has excellent robustness and long-term storage stability.

Additionally, the detection reproducibility was also examined via detecting 5 ng/L DEHP using five identical sensing platforms in the same conditions (Fig. 7C), and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is calculated to be less than 5%. Using the same electrode to measure seven times in parallel in 5 ng/L DEHP solution, and the RSD is 2.7%, demonstrating an excellent reproducibility of the sensing platform. These results support the feasibility of this PEC sensing platform for accurate and selective DEHP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the sensing platform in environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the proposed platform was studied in three environmental samples, including milk, plastic bag and lake water by the standard addition method. The two samples of the milk and plastic bags used in this study were purchased from the local supermarkets. The lake water samples were collected from the campus of Shanxi University. Before measurements, the milk and plastic bag samples were prepared according to the previous report (Fan et al., 2018) with slight modification. For the milk sample, a mixture solution containing 10 mL absolute ethanol and 5 mL milk was treated ultrasonically for 15 min, followed by centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the obtained supernatant was filtered with 0.22 µm filter. For the plastic bag sample, they were first cut into lots of small pieces and cleaned with deionized water. Afterwards, 1 g small pieces were dissolved in 40 mL dichloromethane, and then 100 mL methanol was added to precipitate the polymer material. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifugalized, and the supernatant was filtrated through 0.22 µm filter. The lake water samples were filtered through a 0.22 µm filter to remove suspended particles and other solid impurities. All of the three samples were diluted 100 times with 0.1 M PBS to reduce the interference of complex matrices.

In order to validate the accuracy, the analysis results of the present sensing platform were compared with those obtained by HPLC, summarized in Table 2. It could be seen that the detection results of the proposed method were in good correspondence with HPLC, and the RSD is less than 6.35% (n = 5). The results exhibit high accuracy and feasibility of the proposed method for detection of DEHP in the actual environmental samples. Also, the present method is more time-saving, simpler and low cost compared with HPLC.

In summary, GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs composites were successfully prepared and explored as the photoactive material to develop a sensing platform for monitoring DEHP in the environment. The composites exhibit strong visible absorption ability, good photocurrent responses and PEC activity. Furthermore, the designed PEC sensing platform based on GQDs-decorated TiO2 NTs shows outstanding analytical behavior for DEHP with high sensitivity, selectivity and stability. The proposed sensing platform was utilized successfully to estimate the content of DEHP in the environmental samples. It is anticipated that the present method would afford some new thoughts and novel strategies for development of promising sensing platforms in detecting environmental pollutants.

Yuan Deng: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Wenjun Yan: Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Validation. Yujing Guo: Investigation, Formal analysis. Qiang Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Yingpu Bi: Writing – review & editing. Chuan Dong: Supervision, Validation. Lifang Fan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.
